summary: Julia Evans documents her pre-Hacker School preparation, attempting to implement Snake in C using ncurses as a way to improve her low-level programming skills. The Snake implementation went smoothly, but unit testing in C proved to be the real challenge. After struggling with autotools for hours, she discovered checkmk, a simpler awk-based tool that made writing tests for the check framework painless. She concludes that Snake was too easy and considers writing a shell next.
key_points: Getting started with C unit testing is harder than it should be, with autotools being a major barrier to entry
checkmk simplifies the check unit testing framework by letting you write test snippets that get transformed into proper test code
ncurses is more approachable than expected for building terminal-based games in C
Sometimes the tooling around a task (testing, build systems) is harder than the task itself
money_quotes: I spent an hour or two trying to understand how to autotools and it made me want to kill someone.
The hardest part actually turned out to be unit testing.
One of my goals for this batch is to get better at low-level programming and managing my own memory, so I'm planning to learn C a bit, as well as maybe Go or Rust.
I am also thinking of starting to write about coding on the internet, so here this is.
Conclusion: snake is too easy of a thing to do, so I probably won't take this much further. Next coding day maybe I'll start trying to write a shell?
key_insight: The tooling and infrastructure around C development (build systems, testing frameworks) can be a bigger obstacle than the actual programming, but simpler alternatives exist if you look for them.
